bins.name: State jurisdiction over load interconnection
bins.desc: The VSCC argues that load interconnection has always been a state matter under the Federal Power Act and that FERC has never claimed this jurisdiction. The ANOPR's assertion that large loads are directly on the transmission system is factually wrong. The VSCC opposes exclusive federal jurisdiction and instead wants a cooperative model where FERC sets minimum standards but states retain regulatory control.
quotes: the Commission has never asserted jurisdiction over interconnection of load, large or otherwise. Load interconnection has rather been the exclusive province under the Federal Power Act of state regulators, such as the VSCC.

the best approach to these issues is a cooperative one, where the Commission sets minimum standards for interconnecting utilities to meet, while leaving it to state commissions to regulate these retail tariffs as they have done for many years.

A cooperative federalism solution will also leave the door open for the Commission to assert additional jurisdiction in the future if minimum standards need reform or state-jurisdictional large load queues are not leading to the anticipated efficient outcomes.

the VSCC encourages the Commission to issue in an eventual Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") in this docket a set of minimum standards for load interconnection tariffs that all load serving utilities would have to meet. Responsibility for load interconnection, including maintaining a load interconnection queue and performing necessary load interconnection studies, would remain with the utility and overseen by state regulators.

This assertion is questionable at best, given that virtually all such loads do not directly interconnect to the transmission system, but rather have one or more on site substations to step down the transmission voltage for delivery to the large load.

bins.name: MW threshold invites gaming
bins.desc: The VSCC opposes any fixed MW threshold for a federally run large load queue, arguing it creates incentives for applicants to game the cutoff. A split queue also harms non-large-load customers by separating planning for different load classes.
quotes: Develop a set of Commission-mandated minimum standards for large load interconnections, such as study deposits, site control requirements, and similar criteria while allowing transmission owners, and not the RTOs, who already face long delays and inefficiencies in their existing generation queues, to continue to manage large load interconnection queues with direct oversight of their state regulatory commissions.

The alternative approach envisioned by the ANOPR, which would establish one queue at the RTO for "large loads" and a different queue at the local utility for every other end-use customer still left with the utility, would create planning and study inefficiencies and could potentially disrupt other important loads, like manufacturers and commercial load, from being able to interconnect in a timely manner.

An arbitrary MW-based threshold for a Commission-regulated large load queue could lead to gamesmanship where loads are incentivized to come just above or just below the MW threshold in order to qualify for a state-run or federally-run queue.

bins.name: State deposit and readiness standards
bins.desc: The VSCC supports standardized study deposits and readiness requirements but argues these should be set by FERC as minimum standards and implemented by states, not run through RTO tariffs. Nearly half of states including Virginia are already developing such standards.
quotes: Develop a set of Commission-mandated minimum standards for large load interconnections, such as study deposits, site control requirements, and similar criteria while allowing transmission owners, and not the RTOs, who already face long delays and inefficiencies in their existing generation queues, to continue to manage large load interconnection queues with direct oversight of their state regulatory commissions.

The ANOPR also suggests that "load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties." Like almost half of the states, the VSCC is currently considering new large load tariffs and addressing just those issues.

bins.name: Fast-track co-located and hybrid loads
bins.desc: The VSCC supports accelerated interconnection for co-located and hybrid large loads but argues this should work through the existing generator interconnection queue with a fast-track mechanism, not through a new RTO-managed large load queue.
quotes: The Commission should enable the accelerated interconnection of large loads that are willing to bring their own generation or to be co-located with generation by creating a fast-track generator interconnection queue for those verified generation resources, such as PJM's existing Commission-approved Reliability Resource Initiative.

bins.name: Expedited study for curtailable loads
bins.desc: The VSCC agrees that curtailable and dispatchable loads and hybrid facilities should receive expedited study treatment. Virginia is already holding a technical conference on data center load flexibility and a cooperative approach would let these state efforts continue.
quotes: the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited. Many states, including Virginia, are currently addressing data center load flexibility, and a cooperative approach in this proceeding would allow these efforts to proceed.

bins.name: Utility-run queue and studies
bins.desc: The VSCC argues that load interconnection queues and studies should remain with the utilities that have direct knowledge of the retail system, with state commission oversight. RTO-run queues would duplicate effort, rely on the utility anyway, and introduce regional inefficiencies that harm states with available capacity.
quotes: the best approach to these issues is a cooperative one, where the Commission sets minimum standards for interconnecting utilities to meet, while leaving it to state commissions to regulate these retail tariffs as they have done for many years.

Develop a set of Commission-mandated minimum standards for large load interconnections, such as study deposits, site control requirements, and similar criteria while allowing transmission owners, and not the RTOs, who already face long delays and inefficiencies in their existing generation queues, to continue to manage large load interconnection queues with direct oversight of their state regulatory commissions.

the Commission should acknowledge the inefficiencies inherent to the large, regional queues administered by PJM and other RTOs. These inefficiencies can lead to longer queue lines and projects in states with available capacity being stuck behind projects in states that are already saturated.

The alternative approach envisioned by the ANOPR, which would establish one queue at the RTO for "large loads" and a different queue at the local utility for every other end-use customer still left with the utility, would create planning and study inefficiencies and could potentially disrupt other important loads, like manufacturers and commercial load, from being able to interconnect in a timely manner.

the VSCC encourages the Commission to issue in an eventual Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") in this docket a set of minimum standards for load interconnection tariffs that all load serving utilities would have to meet. Responsibility for load interconnection, including maintaining a load interconnection queue and performing necessary load interconnection studies, would remain with the utility and overseen by state regulators.

bins.name: Co-location via existing generator queue
bins.desc: The VSCC supports fast-tracking co-located load and generation but says this should be done by flagging co-located projects for the RTO to move to an accelerated generator interconnection process, rather than creating a separate large load queue at the RTO. PJM's Reliability Resource Initiative is cited as an approved model.
quotes: The Commission should enable the accelerated interconnection of large loads that are willing to bring their own generation or to be co-located with generation by creating a fast-track generator interconnection queue for those verified generation resources, such as PJM's existing Commission-approved Reliability Resource Initiative.

the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited. Many states, including Virginia, are currently addressing data center load flexibility, and a cooperative approach in this proceeding would allow these efforts to proceed.

bins.name: Load flexibility efforts already underway in states
bins.desc: The VSCC notes that Virginia and many other states are actively working on data center load flexibility and demand-side programs. A cooperative approach would allow these state-level efforts to continue and expand, while a federal takeover would preempt them.
quotes: The Commission should enable the accelerated interconnection of large loads that are willing to bring their own generation or to be co-located with generation by creating a fast-track generator interconnection queue for those verified generation resources, such as PJM's existing Commission-approved Reliability Resource Initiative.

the interconnection study of large loads that agree to be curtailable and hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable should be expedited. Many states, including Virginia, are currently addressing data center load flexibility, and a cooperative approach in this proceeding would allow these efforts to proceed.

bins.name: Integrated load and supply planning at state level
bins.desc: The VSCC contends that keeping load interconnection at the state level best preserves integrated resource planning, where load forecasting and supply adequacy are planned together. Federal takeover would disrupt this in vertically integrated states.
quotes: The shared authority the VSCC proposes would best address resource adequacy concerns, as it would keep load and supply planning together at the state level. This is particularly important in vertically integrated states with robust integrated resource planning regimes.

bins.name: PJM queue inefficiency as cautionary example
bins.desc: The VSCC points to PJM's existing generator interconnection queue as evidence that large regional queues create delays and unfairness across states. Giving PJM a large load queue would replicate these problems. PJM's Reliability Resource Initiative is cited positively as a fast-track model that could serve co-located resources without requiring a new RTO queue.
quotes: Develop a set of Commission-mandated minimum standards for large load interconnections, such as study deposits, site control requirements, and similar criteria while allowing transmission owners, and not the RTOs, who already face long delays and inefficiencies in their existing generation queues, to continue to manage large load interconnection queues with direct oversight of their state regulatory commissions.

the Commission should acknowledge the inefficiencies inherent to the large, regional queues administered by PJM and other RTOs. These inefficiencies can lead to longer queue lines and projects in states with available capacity being stuck behind projects in states that are already saturated.

The Commission should enable the accelerated interconnection of large loads that are willing to bring their own generation or to be co-located with generation by creating a fast-track generator interconnection queue for those verified generation resources, such as PJM's existing Commission-approved Reliability Resource Initiative.

bins.name: Cooperative federalism model
bins.desc: The VSCC's central ask is a cooperative federalism structure: FERC issues minimum standards, utilities implement them in state-filed tariffs, and states retain queue oversight. This avoids jurisdictional litigation, preserves state IRP authority, and keeps the door open for FERC to act further if state outcomes prove inadequate. The VSCC analogizes this to the safe harbor approach in Order No. 888.
quotes: A cooperative federalism solution will also leave the door open for the Commission to assert additional jurisdiction in the future if minimum standards need reform or state-jurisdictional large load queues are not leading to the anticipated efficient outcomes.

the VSCC encourages the Commission to issue in an eventual Notice of Proposed Rulemaking ("NOPR") in this docket a set of minimum standards for load interconnection tariffs that all load serving utilities would have to meet. Responsibility for load interconnection, including maintaining a load interconnection queue and performing necessary load interconnection studies, would remain with the utility and overseen by state regulators.

The ANOPR also suggests that "load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties." Like almost half of the states, the VSCC is currently considering new large load tariffs and addressing just those issues.

bins.name: Resource adequacy and IRP protection
bins.desc: The VSCC argues that state-level integrated resource planning processes, which link load forecasting to supply adequacy, would be threatened by federal jurisdiction over load interconnection. Keeping load interconnection with states preserves this planning coherence, especially in vertically integrated states.
quotes: The shared authority the VSCC proposes would best address resource adequacy concerns, as it would keep load and supply planning together at the state level. This is particularly important in vertically integrated states with robust integrated resource planning regimes.
